$comment: Seed corpus, not a waist artifact. Grounds the rights-guard's abstract '30 UDHR invariants' (the integers in a manifest's rights_guard.invariants) in real UK law: each UDHR article mapped to its European Convention right and the Human Rights Act 1998 provision that domesticates it. Legislation verified via the legislation.gov.uk / Lex API (HRA 1998 = ukpga/1998/42, retrieved 2026-07; Act status 'revised', valid 2026-04-06). HONEST FINDING surfaced by building this: the HRA/ECHR domesticates the CIVIL-POLITICAL rights but not the socio-economic ones — the UDHR articles on social security, work, standard of living and culture (22-27, bar the education article 26) have NO Convention or HRA counterpart, and neither do movement/asylum/nationality (13-15). 20 of 30 articles have some domestic legal backing, 10 have none — and 3 of the 20 (articles 1 dignity, 6 recognition-before-law, 8 effective-remedy) are backed only indirectly/structurally, with no single Convention article to quote, so they carry no retrieved text. Articles 28-29 are among the unbacked (programmatic). So the UK, expressed on the kernel's UDHR lattice (§6), sits at a partial point by law, not by choice. That is exactly the kind of subtraction a §6 conformance manifest is built to declare.

meta.name: United Kingdom
meta.remedy_note: The HRA gives courts only a s.4 DECLARATION OF INCOMPATIBILITY: the offending law is flagged but stays in force, and Parliament — sovereign — may proceed regardless. Courts cannot strike primary legislation, and no part of the constitution is unamendable. The compatibility gate (s.19) runs before Second Reading, but it too only reports; it does not block.
meta.scope_note: The Human Rights Act applies to all UK primary legislation — a court may review any Act for Convention compatibility. Scope is unconditional; the weakness here is the remedy (only a s.4 declaration), not applicability.

proto_rights_guard.note: A Minister in charge of a Bill must, BEFORE Second Reading, state whether in their view the Bill is compatible with the Convention rights (or that the government wishes to proceed regardless). This is a real, shipped, statutory PROTO-RIGHTS-GUARD: a compatibility check gated ahead of the decision — SVC-3 (§3.3) as legislation already practised, minus the machine-checkability. s.4 (declaration of incompatibility) is its judicial-review escalation — courts flag, never strike, exactly the guard's 'escalate, never veto' posture.

invariants.udhr: Art 1 — free and equal in dignity and rights
invariants.right: (foundational; expressed through Art 14)
ECHR Art 14 — prohibition of discrimination
invariants.text: Article 14 Prohibition of discrimination The enjoyment of the rights and freedoms set forth in this Convention shall be secured without discrimination on any ground such as sex, race, colour, language, religion, political or other opinion, national or social origin, association with a national minor

invariants.right: ECHR Art 2 (life) + Art 5 (liberty & security)
invariants.text: Article 2 Right to life 1) Everyone’s right to life shall be protected by law. No one shall be deprived of his life intentionally save in the execution of a sentence of a court following his conviction of a crime for which this penalty is provided by law.

invariants.right: ECHR Art 4 — prohibition of slavery and forced labour
invariants.text: Article 4 Prohibition of slavery and forced labour 1) No one shall be held in slavery or servitude. 2) No one shall be required to perform forced or compulsory labour.

invariants.right: ECHR Art 3 — prohibition of torture
invariants.text: Article 3 Prohibition of torture No one shall be subjected to torture or to inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment.

invariants.right: (implicit; via Art 6 fair trial)

invariants.udhr: Art 7 — equal protection of the law
invariants.right: ECHR Art 14 — prohibition of discrimination
invariants.text: Article 14 Prohibition of discrimination The enjoyment of the rights and freedoms set forth in this Convention shall be secured without discrimination on any ground such as sex, race, colour, language, religion, political or other opinion, national or social origin, association with a national minor

invariants.right: ECHR Art 13 (given effect by HRA ss.7-8 remedies)
ECHR Art 5 — right to liberty and security
invariants.text: Article 5 Right to liberty and security 1) Everyone has the right to liberty and security of person.

invariants.right: ECHR Art 6 — right to a fair trial
invariants.text: Article 6 Right to a fair trial 1) In the determination of his civil rights and obligations or of any criminal charge against him, everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal established by law.

invariants.udhr: Art 11 — presumption of innocence; no retroactive law
invariants.right: ECHR Art 6(2) + Art 7 — no punishment without law
invariants.text: Article 7 No punishment without law 1) No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national or international law at the time when it was committed.

invariants.right: ECHR Art 8 — respect for private and family life
invariants.text: Article 8 Right to respect for private and family life 1) Everyone has the right to respect for his private and family life, his home and his correspondence.

invariants.right: ECHR Protocol 4 Art 2 (NOT ratified into HRA by the UK)
(no direct Convention right)
(no direct Convention right)
ECHR Art 12 — right to marry
invariants.text: Article 12 Right to marry Men and women of marriageable age have the right to marry and to found a family, according to the national laws governing the exercise of this right.

invariants.right: ECHR Protocol 1 Art 1 — protection of property
invariants.text: Article 1 Protection of property Every natural or legal person is entitled to the peaceful enjoyment of his possessions. No one shall be deprived of his possessions except in the public interest and subject to the conditions provided for by law and by the general principles of international law.

invariants.right: ECHR Art 9 — freedom of thought, conscience and religion
invariants.text: Article 9 Freedom of thought, conscience and religion 1) Everyone has the right to freedom of thought, conscience and religion; this right includes freedom to change his religion or belief and freedom, either alone or in community with others and in public or private, to manifest his religion or be

invariants.right: ECHR Art 10 — freedom of expression
invariants.citation: ukpga/1998/42 Sch. 1 Pt I Art 10; procedural safeguard at s.12
invariants.text: Article 10 Freedom of expression 1) Everyone has the right to freedom of expression. This right shall include freedom to hold opinions and to receive and impart information and ideas without interference by public authority and regardless of frontiers.

invariants.right: ECHR Art 11 — freedom of assembly and association
invariants.text: Article 11 Freedom of assembly and association 1) Everyone has the right to freedom of peaceful assembly and to freedom of association with others, including the right to form and to join trade unions for the protection of his interests.

invariants.udhr: Art 21 — take part in government; free elections
invariants.right: ECHR Protocol 1 Art 3 — right to free elections
invariants.text: Article 3 Right to free elections The High Contracting Parties undertake to hold free elections at reasonable intervals by secret ballot, under conditions which will ensure the free expression of the opinion of the people in the choice of the legislature.

invariants.right: (no Convention right — socio-economic)
(association element only, via Art 11; no economic right)
(no Convention right — socio-economic)

invariants.udhr: Art 25 — adequate standard of living, health
invariants.right: (no Convention right — socio-economic)
ECHR Protocol 1 Art 2 — right to education
invariants.text: Article 2 Right to education No person shall be denied the right to education.

invariants.right: (no Convention right — socio-economic/cultural)
(programmatic; no justiciable Convention right)
(reflected in the qualified-rights limitation clauses of Arts 8-11)
ECHR Art 17 — prohibition of abuse of rights
invariants.text: Article 17 Prohibition of abuse of rights Nothing in this Convention may be interpreted as implying for any State, group or person any right to engage in any activity or perform any act aimed at the destruction of any of the rights and freedoms set forth herein or at their limitation to a greater ex
